perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, NAI and Co-Host desire to place a Co-Host "hot link" for the Goods at the Originating Locations and NAI and Co-Host desire to enter into certain additional agreements regarding such marketing opportunities through the Originating Locations.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, NAI and Co-Host desire to place a Co-Host "hot link" for the Products at the Originating Locations and NAI and Co-Host desire to enter into certain additional agreements regarding such marketing opportunities through the Originating Locations.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Goods' has been changed to 'Products' in the Recitals section. This creates potential confusion because 'Goods' is explicitly defined in the first recital on page 1, while 'Products' is defined in Exhibit 'E' within the Distribution Addendum and section 9b, creating ambiguity as to whether the hotlink encompasses all products or only those defined as 'Goods.' This difference in definition can lead to disputes regarding the scope of the marketing agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, NAI owns and/or markets certain computer software and hardware products set forth on Exhibit "A" ("Products"). WHEREAS, Co-Host is an independent reseller of computer products to end users ordering products through web sites on the Internet operated by Co-Host. WHEREAS, Co-Host distributes electronic copies of the Products pursuant to the ESD Agreement. WHEREAS, Co-Host desires to distribute the Products and NAI desires to make the Products available to Co-Host for further distribution. THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, and of the mutual covenants and agreements hereinafter set forth, NAI and Co-Host enter into the following additional agreements regarding the Products:
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Goods' to 'Products' introduces ambiguity because both terms are defined differently within the contract. The original text specifically refers to 'Goods,' which is defined in the first recital on page 1. The modified text introduces 'Products,' which is defined in Exhibit 'E' within the Distribution Addendum and section 9b. The contradicted text defines 'Products' as 'certain computer software and hardware products set forth on Exhibit "A".' This difference in definition leads to an in-text contradiction, as the scope of the 'hot link' becomes unclear. It is uncertain whether the marketing agreement now encompasses all products, only those defined as 'Goods,' or only those listed on Exhibit 'A', creating ambiguity and potentially leading to disputes regarding the scope of the marketing agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products ("Software") at the Originating Locations.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of applications ("Software") at the Originating Locations.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'software products' was changed to 'applications'. While 'Software' is defined as software products, the change to 'applications' introduces ambiguity. 'Software products' typically refers to complete, marketable software packages. 'Applications' can mean smaller, less comprehensive software components, leaving room for interpretation as to what Co-Host has exclusive rights to sell. This change creates uncertainty and can be contradicted to sections where software products are being reffered.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exclusive Positioning. Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products ("Software") at the Originating Locations. The preceding sentence shall not prohibit NAI from (i) reselling Software including NAI Goods (hereinafter defined) from the McAfee Mall; (ii) referencing and linking to sites of strategic partners (other than competitors of Co-Host listed on Part 2 of Exhibit "C") which may also be involved in the resale of Software from such sites; provided that no Software may be purchased on the page of such site which is linked to any Originating Location and further provided that the references and links to the sites of strategic partners shall not be placed on the Online Service Page; and (iii) advertising Software with banners, buttons and other forms of online advertising; provided that any link from such advertising takes the end user to the publisher of the Software and not a reseller of Goods (other than Co-Host). For example, an advertising banner or button for the Windows 98 software program may link back to the website of Microsoft Corporation but not the website of Dell Computer Corporation which is reselling the program. Without limitation on the foregoing, NAI may co-host a comparative shopping service on the Originating Locations. NAI will obtain a written covenant that the comparative shopping co-host will present sellers of Software in a neutral manner and upon request of Co-Host will require removal or alteration of presentations by such co-host on such co-hosted facility which Co-Host reasonably deems to be non-neutral; provided, however, that, notwithstanding the foregoing, Co-Host may be the featured or most prominent Software reseller on any such service.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'software products', while the changed text uses 'applications'. The explanation clarifies that this shift introduces ambiguity, as 'applications' can be interpreted more narrowly than 'software products'. This directly contradicts the section 'Exclusive Positioning', which grants Co-Host exclusive rights to resell 'software products'. If 'applications' is interpreted as something less than 'software products', NAI could argue that it is not restricted from reselling software that falls within the 'software products' definition but not the 'applications' definition, thus contradicting the exclusivity granted to Co-Host for 'software products'.

perturbation.original_text: NAI will obtain a written covenant that the comparative shopping co-host will present sellers of Software in a neutral manner and upon request of Co-Host will require removal or alteration of presentations by such co-host on such co-hosted facility which Co-Host reasonably deems to be non-neutral; provided, however, that, notwithstanding the foregoing, Co-Host may be the featured or most prominent Software reseller on any such service.
perturbation.changed_text: NAI will obtain a written covenant that the comparative shopping co-host will present vendors of Software in a neutral manner and upon request of Co-Host will require removal or alteration of presentations by such co-host on such co-hosted facility which Co-Host reasonably deems to be non-neutral; provided, however, that, notwithstanding the foregoing, Co-Host may be the featured or most prominent Software provider on any such service.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'sellers' was changed to 'vendors' and 'reseller' to 'provider'. These terms are used interchangeably but without explicit definition or cross-reference. This introduces ambiguity about the roles and responsibilities of entities involved in distributing 'Software', especially considering Co-Host is defined as a 'reseller' elsewhere in the agreement. Changing 'reseller' to 'provider' makes it unclear if Co-Host's role is simply a provider to end-users or a distributor, creating uncertainty in the interpretation of this clause and potentially contradicting the intended limitations set forth in the original text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exclusive Positioning. Co-Host shall be the exclusive reseller of software products ("Software") at the Originating Locations. The preceding sentence shall not prohibit NAI from (i) reselling Software including NAI Goods (hereinafter defined) from the McAfee Mall; (ii) referencing and linking to sites of strategic partners (other than competitors of Co-Host listed on Part 2 of Exhibit "C") which may also be involved in the resale of Software from such sites; provided that no Software may be purchased on the page of such site which is linked to any Originating Location and further provided that the references and links to the sites of strategic partners shall not be placed on the Online Service Page; and (iii) advertising Software with banners, buttons and other forms of online advertising; provided that any link from such advertising takes the end user to the publisher of the Software and not a reseller of Goods (other than Co-Host). For example, an advertising banner or button for the Windows 98 software program may link back to the website of Microsoft Corporation but not the website of Dell Computer Corporation which is reselling the program. Without limitation on the foregoing, NAI may co-host a comparative shopping service on the Originating Locations. NAI will obtain a written covenant that the comparative shopping co-host will present vendors of Software in a neutral manner and upon request of Co-Host will require removal or alteration of presentations by such co-host on such co-hosted facility which Co-Host reasonably deems to be non-neutral; provided, however, that, notwithstanding the foregoing, Co-Host may be the featured or most prominent Software provider on any such service.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'sellers' and 'resellers' with a degree of implied specificity regarding their role in the distribution chain. The changed text replaces these with 'vendors' and 'providers,' respectively. Given that the contradicted text defines Co-Host as the 'exclusive reseller of software products,' changing 'reseller' to 'provider' creates ambiguity about Co-Host's role, potentially contradicting their exclusive reseller status. The change impacts the exclusive reseller agreement since, without specific definitions, it is unclear whether Co-Host is simply a provider to end-users or a distributor, thereby weakening the restrictions set forth in the original text regarding NAI's ability to co-host comparative shopping services. This constitutes a misaligned terminology leading to an in-text contradiction.
